role_summary: SNCC organizer and photographer in Alabama and Mississippi from 1963; staff photographer for Black Star photo agency; co-founder (1981) of Ganados del Valle, the New Mexico rural Hispanic cooperative; MacArthur Foundation fellow, 1990.
ai_reading: Varela's top semantic neighbors are Betty Garman Robinson (entry 7, count 132), Dr. Doris Derby (entry 32), Charles McLaurin (entry 17), Martha Prescod Norman Noonan (entry 83), and John Churchville (entry 63). The cluster is the SNCC-allied-organizers-and-photographers cohort. The embedding therefore reads her as a SNCC voice rather than as a Chicana voice the cultural reading often foregrounds (Varela is one of the relatively few Latina figures in the civil rights movement record). Her Individual Conscience vs Collective Discipline position at -0.42 reads her testimony toward Individual Conscience, an unexpected pull for a photographer whose post-SNCC work organized rural Hispanic cooperatives (Ganados del Valle from 1981), which is by definition collective enterprise; the embedding may be catching the documentarian's eye in her transcript more than the organizer's hand.
biographical_paragraph: Varela was born in January 1940 in Pennsylvania and joined SNCC in 1963, working in Alabama and Mississippi as both an organizer and a photographer; her Black Star photo-agency credentials placed her work in the periodicals of the period [src: 2]. Julian Bond's 2011 University Press of Mississippi book This Light of Ours: Activist Photographers of the Civil Rights Movement documents her photographic work alongside that of her SNCC colleagues [src: 2]. In 1981 she co-founded Ganados del Valle, a wool-and-sheep cooperative in northern New Mexico that has continued the rural Hispanic land-grant tradition through her organizing; in 1990 she received a MacArthur Foundation fellowship in recognition of that work [src: 3]. Her Library of Congress oral history is entry 81 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].
movement_context: Varela came into the civil rights movement through the Students for a Democratic Society Port Huron founding cohort in June 1962, where she was among the small group of student activists who drafted and ratified the Port Huron Statement at the United Auto Workers retreat in Port Huron, Michigan; the founding cohort was overwhelmingly white and male, and Varela's presence as a Latina participant inside the early SDS leadership was an early instance of a pattern that recurs across her movement biography, that of one of the few Latina organizers inside an institutional structure framed by Black-and-white movement demographics [src: 4, 5]. By 1963 she had transferred to SNCC as a field staff member, recruited by James Forman and Bob Moses to bring photographic-documentation capability to the Mississippi and Alabama field operations; the Black Star photo agency credential she carried gave her work the institutional credibility to run in mainstream periodicals of the period, expanding the readership reach of the SNCC documentary record beyond the movement press [src: 4]. Across 1963-1968 Varela worked the Mississippi Delta projects, the Selma voter registration drive, and the Alabama Black Belt field offices, producing the photographic record of SNCC's Southern field operations alongside Dr. Doris Derby (catalog entry 32), the other SNCC staff photographer working the same period; the two were the field-photography cohort SNCC sent into the Deep South to document the voter-registration projects, the Freedom Schools, the Freedom House daily life, and the law-enforcement and Klan surveillance the field workers faced [src: 4, 6]. In Selma during the spring of 1965, Varela's camera documented the voter-registration organizing that culminated in the March 7 Bloody Sunday attack on the Edmund Pettus Bridge and the subsequent March 21-25 Selma-to-Montgomery march under federal protection; her photographs sit alongside the FBI, AP, and Black Star coverage that established the visual record of the campaign [src: 7]. In 1968, as SNCC's institutional cohesion fractured and the Black Power turn made non-Black field staff increasingly out of position inside the organization, Varela moved to New Mexico and began the second long arc of her movement career, organizing alongside Reies Lopez Tijerina's Alianza Federal de Mercedes (the New Mexico Chicano land-grant movement that had taken over the Rio Arriba County courthouse at Tierra Amarilla in June 1967) and embedding inside the rural Hispanic land-grant communities of the upper Chama River valley [src: 8]. The 1981 co-founding of Ganados del Valle and the Tierra Wools weaving cooperative in Los Ojos, New Mexico, with Antonio Manzanares and other land-grant community members, built a cooperative institutional infrastructure for the Navajo-Churro sheep, traditional Rio Grande-style weaving, and the rural Hispanic economy of northern New Mexico; the cooperative's grazing-rights work brought it into long-running conflict with the U.S. Forest Service and the Sierra Club through the late 1980s and early 1990s [src: 8]. The 1990 MacArthur Fellowship cited the cooperative-building work, naming Varela as one of the rural community organizers whose post-1968 career extended the SNCC field-organizing methodology into a different demographic and ecological terrain than the Deep South of her first movement decade [src: 3].
legacy_and_reception: Varela has continued her New Mexico organizing work through Rural Resources, the nonprofit she founded to extend the Ganados del Valle community-development model into other rural Hispanic communities of the upper Rio Grande basin and the southern Rocky Mountain region; the work has run from her Los Ojos base for more than four decades since the 1981 cooperative founding [src: 8]. Her 2008 book Time It Was: American Stories from the Sixties (Pearson Prentice Hall), edited by Karen Manners Smith and Tim Koster, includes her photographic contributions to the volume's documentary record of the period [src: 9]. Her contributing essay to the 2010 University of Illinois Press volume Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC (edited by Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, Judy Richardson, Betty Garman Robinson, Jean Smith Young, and Dorothy M. Zellner) places her testimony alongside the fifty other women SNCC field staff whose collected memoir is the standard primary-source compilation of the women-of-SNCC record; the volume includes her account of the 1963-1968 field-photography work [src: 10]. Her photography monograph Encuentro: Maria Varela / SNCC (Albuquerque Museum, 2018) collected the SNCC-period photographs into a museum-catalogue treatment with Spanish-language apparatus, placing the Deep South field record inside a Chicana-photographic-history framework that the AI's-reading observation above flags as a less common reading of her work than the SNCC-photographer-cohort embedding makes [src: 11]. The Library of Congress oral history at catalog entry 81, recorded in 2013 for the Civil Rights History Project (AFC 2010/039), and her presence in the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture's collection of SNCC field photography round out the institutional archival record [src: 1].

gallery.alt: Participants carrying American flags during the 1965 civil rights march from Selma to Montgomery, Alabama
gallery.caption: Participants, some carrying American flags, marching from Selma to Montgomery, Alabama, in the federally protected March 21-25, 1965 voting rights march that followed the March 7 Bloody Sunday attack on the Edmund Pettus Bridge. Varela was in Selma during the spring 1965 voter-registration campaign as a SNCC field photographer, and her camera documented the organizing work that culminated in this march and the subsequent passage of the Voting Rights Act. Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (Digital ID cph.3c33090).
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from Library of Congress)
gallery.license: Public domain (published in the United States between 1931 and 1977 without a copyright notice)

gallery.alt: Alabama state troopers attacking civil rights demonstrators on the Edmund Pettus Bridge, March 7, 1965
gallery.caption: Federal Bureau of Investigation photograph of Alabama state troopers attacking demonstrators on the Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma, Alabama, on March 7, 1965, the event known as Bloody Sunday. The attack, captured by FBI, AP, and Black Star photographers, drew national television coverage and forced the federal intervention that produced the protected March 21-25 Selma-to-Montgomery march. Varela's field photography in Selma during the spring 1965 voter-registration campaign sits alongside the institutional photographic record this image represents.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from National Park Service Civil Rights travel website)
gallery.license: Public domain (work product of a federal government agency)

gallery.alt: A horned Navajo-Churro ram on rangeland in the American Southwest
gallery.caption: A Navajo-Churro ram, the heritage sheep breed central to the rural Hispanic and Native sheep-ranching economy of northern New Mexico that the Ganados del Valle cooperative was founded in 1981 to sustain. The cooperative's traditional Rio Grande-style weaving work, organized through the Tierra Wools enterprise in Los Ojos, New Mexico, runs on the Navajo-Churro fleece, and the cooperative's grazing-rights advocacy across the 1980s and 1990s brought it into long-running conflict with the U.S. Forest Service and the Sierra Club over public-lands grazing access.

sources.title: Clayborne Carson, In Struggle: SNCC and the Black Awakening of the 1960s

sources.publisher: Yale University Press, 1978; WorldCat OCLC 57346030 (the canonical historical treatment of the 1965 Selma voting-rights campaign, the March 7 Bloody Sunday attack on the Edmund Pettus Bridge, and the federally protected March 21-25 Selma-to-Montgomery march that Varela's field photography documented)
